worth_it_explanation: Deals running through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) carry negotiated rates that usually sit above what the open market pays, so a deal that stalls or one whose floor is set wrong costs the publisher the premium the deal was meant to capture. A deal ID that stopped delivering, or a deal floor that lets the open market undercut a guaranteed rate, can drain value without showing up on a dashboard anyone reads daily. The check reads the deal status and the floor and priority settings the publisher already has in the APS console and the ad server, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific deal ID or a concrete floor value the publisher can act on.
intro: APS connects publishers to programmatic deals through Transparent Ad Marketplace (TAM) and Unified Ad Marketplace (UAM), where a buyer negotiates a Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal that delivers against the publisher's inventory at an agreed rate. Two things decide whether those deals earn their value. The first is health, meaning the deal IDs are active and delivering rather than stalled at zero. The second is the floor and priority setup, meaning the deal clears at the rate it was negotiated for rather than being undercut by the open market or undercutting it. This subcategory is about whether the publisher monitors deal delivery and whether the floors and priorities are aligned so deals and the open auction sit in the right order. These checks confirm that a deal which was set up is still working and that it is priced to protect the premium it was meant to carry.

audit_checks.title: Deal delivery is not monitored, so stalled deal IDs go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal carries a rate the publisher negotiated above the open market, so a deal ID that stalls at zero delivery costs that premium every day it sits unwatched. Buyers pause deals, change targeting, or shift budget without telling the publisher, and a deal that delivered last month can stop this month. Without a regular read of deal status and spend, the publisher learns that a deal died only when the revenue is already gone.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal in Amazon Publisher Services (APS) is an agreement between the publisher and a buyer that carries its own deal ID and an agreed rate. A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal invites a named buyer into an auction at a negotiated floor, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal commits the buyer to a fixed volume at a fixed price. In both cases the rate usually sits above what the open market pays, which is the whole point of striking the deal, so the value of the deal depends on it actually delivering impressions.

Deals do not stay healthy on their own. A buyer can pause a deal, narrow its targeting, exhaust its budget, or move spend to another publisher, and none of those changes send the publisher a notice. A deal ID that delivered well one month can drop to zero the next while the line item still sits active in the ad server, looking ready to receive a bid that no longer comes. The deal appears to be in place, but the premium it was meant to capture has stopped arriving.

In a Prebid-compatible setup, APS deals surface through deal-specific targeting keys such as hb_deal_BIDDER, and Prebid's deals guidance describes how those keys map to line items in the ad server. That mapping is what lets a publisher break out delivery by deal ID and see which deals are spending and which have gone quiet. Reading deal status, impressions, and spend on a regular cadence, rather than only at renewal, is what turns a stalled deal into something the publisher catches in days rather than discovering at the end of the quarter.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular report breaks out delivery and spend by deal ID, so a deal that drops to zero is not flagged
  • Deal line items sit active in the ad server with no recent delivery and no one has noticed
  • The publisher cannot say, without checking, which of its active deals delivered in the last week
You may want to confirm that deal delivery is read on a regular cadence rather than only at renewal. One approach is to build a recurring report broken out by deal ID with impressions and spend, then set a threshold that flags any active deal whose delivery has fallen to zero.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, deals are often struck against premium placements or specific sections that a buyer wants for brand reasons, so a stalled deal on a homepage or section-front unit leaves high-value inventory clearing at open-market rates instead. You may want to watch delivery on the placements tied to named deals closely since those are the units where a quiet deal costs the most per impression.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may be tied to product or category pages where a buyer wants audience close to a purchase, and that inventory clears higher than editorial, so a stalled deal there forfeits the premium on the publisher's most valuable pages. You may want to break out deal delivery by the commercial inventory specifically rather than reading a blended number that the larger editorial inventory dominates.

learn_more.source_title: Deals for Header Bidding in Prebid Server

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed deal reporting and deal-management documentation for Transparent Ad Marketplace and Unified Ad Marketplace is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active deals running through APS, then look for whether their delivery and spend are read on a regular cadence and whether any active deal has stalled at or near zero.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the deals or marketplace section of the APS console and list the active PMP and PG deals with their deal IDs, buyers, and negotiated rates.
For each deal, note the recent delivery and spend the console reports, and flag any deal showing zero or near-zero delivery while still marked active.
Ask the publisher how often deal delivery is reviewed and whether any alert or threshold flags a deal that stops delivering.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID, or by the deal-targeted line items, with impressions, revenue, and the date of last delivery.
Compare each active deal's recent delivery against its history to see which deals are spending and which have gone quiet.
Record any deal line item that sits active with no recent delivery since that is a deal the publisher believes is working but is not.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active deal with its deal ID, recent delivery, and spend, alongside how often delivery is reviewed. A setup where deal delivery is read on a regular cadence and stalled deals are caught and acted on passes. A setup where no recurring report breaks out delivery by deal ID, or where an active deal has stalled at zero without anyone noticing, is a fail recorded with the deal IDs affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the active deals with their delivery and spend, and how often they are reviewed, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor programmatic deal delivery on a regular cadence, so a stalled deal ID can go unnoticed until renewal. Deals running through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) carry rates negotiated above the open market, and a buyer can pause or narrow a deal without notice, so a deal that delivered last month can drop to zero this month while its line item still sits active. {{voice_recommend}} building a recurring report broken out by deal ID with impressions and spend, and setting a threshold that flags any active deal whose delivery has fallen to zero. After that report is in place, review it on a fixed cadence so a deal that stops delivering is caught in days rather than at the end of the quarter.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are not aligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor and its line-item priority decide where a deal sits against the open auction. Set the deal floor too low and the open market can clear a deal-eligible impression below the rate the publisher negotiated. Set the priority so a deal outranks higher open-market bids without cause, and the publisher trades away a better price to honor a deal that was not the best bid. Either misalignment moves money in the wrong direction on inventory the deal touches.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal sits in the same auction as the open market, so the publisher's settings decide how the two compete. Two settings carry that decision. The deal floor is the minimum price the deal must meet, and the line-item priority is where the deal ranks against other line items in the ad server when more than one is eligible. When those two are aligned with the open market, a deal wins when it should and the open auction wins when it offers more. When they are not, the publisher loses value on one side or the other.

Consider the two ways the alignment breaks. If the deal floor is set below the open-market clearing price for that inventory, an impression the deal could have filled at its negotiated rate can instead clear in the open auction at a lower price, so the publisher collects less than the deal promised. If the priority is set so the deal outranks open-market demand regardless of price, the deal can win an impression that an open-market bidder valued more highly, so the publisher honors the deal at the cost of a better bid. The aim is an order where a guaranteed deal is protected for the volume it committed to while a price-priority deal still has to beat the open market on price.

Prebid's deals guidance is explicit on this point. It describes deal line items receiving a higher priority than open-market line items, while typically ranking below directly sold inventory, and it covers the 'preferdeals' option that prioritizes a deal over non-deal bids. Those controls are what let a publisher place a deal in the right position rather than leaving it at a default that does not reflect the negotiated terms. The audit question is whether the deal floor and priority in place match what the deal was negotiated to deliver, or whether the deal is fighting the open market in a way that loses money.

A few signs point to deal floors or priorities being misaligned:
  • A deal floor sits below the open-market clearing price for the same inventory, so the deal can be undercut by the open auction
  • A price-priority (PMP) deal is set to outrank open-market demand regardless of price, so it can win impressions a higher open-market bid valued more
  • Programmatic Guaranteed deals are not prioritized to meet their committed volume, so guaranteed delivery competes on price it should not have to
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor and priority match the terms it was negotiated under. One approach is to compare each deal's floor against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory, then set priorities so guaranteed deals are protected for their volume while price-priority deals still have to win on price.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements often carry both a negotiated deal and strong open-market demand, so the floor and priority on those units decide which side wins a contested impression. You may want to check that deal floors on premium placements sit at or above the open-market clearing price there, so a deal protects the premium rather than letting the open auction undercut the negotiated rate.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can draw high open-market bids because the audience is close to a purchase, so a deal floor set too low on that inventory risks the open market clearing it below the deal's rate. You may want to compare deal floors against the open-market clearing price on the commercial pages specifically since that is where the gap between a negotiated rate and a strong open bid is widest.

learn_more.source_title: Deals for Header Bidding in Prebid Server

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed documentation for deal floor and priority configuration in Transparent Ad Marketplace and Unified Ad Marketplace is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor and line-item priority, then compare them against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory to see whether the deal sits in the right position.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the deals section of the APS console and list each active deal with its type (PMP or PG), its negotiated rate, and its deal floor.
Note for each deal whether its floor is set to the negotiated rate or to a lower value that the open market could clear below.
Record the deal type for each because a Programmatic Guaranteed deal and a price-priority Private Marketplace deal call for different priority treatment.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Build a report that shows the open-market average clearing price (eCPM) for the same inventory each deal targets.
Compare each deal's floor against the open-market clearing price for that inventory to see whether the deal could be undercut or is set to undercut a higher open bid.

Review the line-item priority for each deal against the priority of open-market and directly sold line items, and record any case where a price-priority deal outranks open-market demand regardless of price.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each deal's type, floor, and line-item priority alongside the open-market clearing price for the same inventory. A setup where guaranteed deals are protected for their committed volume, price-priority deals still have to beat the open market on price, and deal floors sit at or above the open-market clearing price passes. A setup where a deal floor sits below the open-market price, or a price-priority deal outranks higher open bids regardless of price, is a fail recorded with the deals and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor and priority alongside the open-market clearing price for the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that deal floors and priorities at {{site_name}} are not aligned with the open market, so deals and the open auction are not competing in the right order. A deal floor set below the open-market clearing price lets the open auction undercut a negotiated rate, and a price-priority deal set to outrank open-market demand regardless of price can win an impression a higher open bid valued more. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each deal's floor against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory in Amazon Publisher Services (APS), then setting priorities so Programmatic Guaranteed deals are protected for their committed volume while price-priority deals still have to win on price. After the change, recheck how deals and the open market clear against each other to confirm each deal sits in the position its terms call for.
